'''Albert Jan "Ap" Dijksterhuis''' (born 12 November 1968, [[Zutphen]])<ref>[http://albumacademicum.uva.nl/id/id002616 Prof. dr. A.J. Dijksterhuis, 1968 -] at the UvA ''Album Academicum website''.</ref> received his Ph.D. in social sciences from [[Radboud University Nijmegen]] in 1996. His adviser was Ad van Knippenberg. From 1996-1999, he did post-doc work as a research fellow of the [[Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences|Royal Dutch Academy of Arts and Sciences]], located in Amsterdam.  In 2000, he became a professor at the [[University of Amsterdam]], returning to Radboud University Nijmegen in 2006 where he stayed until 2025. In 2007, his first book was published, in Dutch, called ‘Het slimme onbewuste’ (‘The Smart Unconscious’).<ref>{{cite web|title=Unconscious Lab - Ap Dijksterhuis |url=http://www.unconsciouslab.com/index.php?page=People&subpage=Ap%20Dijksterhuis |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131209220245/http://unconsciouslab.com/index.php?page=People&subpage=Ap%20Dijksterhuis |archivedate=December 9, 2013 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Radboud University - Ap Dijksterhuis|url=http://www.ru.nl/socialpsychology/faculty/prof_dr_ap/|archive-url=https://archive.today/20140529170547/http://www.ru.nl/socialpsychology/faculty/prof_dr_ap/|url-status=dead|archive-date=2014-05-29}}</ref>

==Awards==
*2005- Award for Distinguished Scientific Early Career Contributions to Psychology - [[American Psychological Association|APA]]<ref>[https://psycnet.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037%2F0003-066X.60.8.799 Albert Jan (Ap) Dijksterhuis: Award for Distinguished Scientific Early Career Contributions to Psychology. - PsycNet]</ref>
*2005- [[Kurt Lewin]] Medal - [[European Association of Social Psychology|EASP]]<ref>[https://www.easp.eu/about/awards/kurt-lewin-medals/ Kurt Lewin Medal|European Association of Social Psychology]</ref>
*2007- Wegner Theoretical Innovation Prize - [[Society for Personality and Social Psychology|SPSP]]<ref>[https://www.spsp.org/awards/annualawards/outstanding-contributions/wegner-theoretical-innovation-prize Wegner Theoretical Innovation Prize|SPSP]</ref>

==Research==
His areas of research have varied, but all deal with [[Unconscious mind|unconscious]]: the perception-behavior link, goals, [http://supportseekers.info/self-esteem/ Implicit Self Esteem Issues], and unconscious thought. His theory that certain behaviour can be modified by unconscious cues is somewhat controversial.<ref>Alison Abbott, [http://www.nature.com/news/disputed-results-a-fresh-blow-for-social-psychology-1.12902 Disputed results a fresh blow for social psychology], Nature, 30 April 2013.</ref>

===Perception-Behavior link (1995–2001)===
[[John Bargh|Bargh]], Chen, and Burrows showed a relationship between activation of traits and behavior. In one of the experiments they [[Priming (psychology)|primed]] participants with words related to the elderly and found they walked slower leaving the experiment than [[Scientific control|controls]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Bargh|first=John A.|author2=Chen, Mark |author3=Burrows, Lara |title=Automaticity of social behavior: Direct effects of trait construct and stereotype activation on action.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=1996|volume=71|issue=2|pages=230–244|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.71.2.230|pmid=8765481}}</ref>  Dijksterhuis started investigating the link between perception and behavior, but instead of direct behavior he wanted to measure ability. Over multiple studies, Dijksterhuis and van Knippenberg primed subjects with the stereotype of professor (thought of as intelligent), and the stereotype of soccer hooligans (thought of as stupid). In the last study, they also primed them directly with intelligent or stupid, rather than just using a stereotype. In a supposedly unrelated task, they used a general knowledge measure to test for effects of the priming. In all of the studies, participants primed with intelligence, by stereotype or directly, did better than controls. The opposite is true for those primed with stupidity. The duration of priming (9 minutes vs 2 minutes) affected performance with those in the long condition performing better than those in the short condition.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Dijksterhuis|first=Ap|author2=van Knippenberg, Ad|title=The relation between perception and behavior, or how to win a game of Trivial Pursuit.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|volume=74|issue=4|pages=865–877|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.74.4.865|pmid=9569649|date=April 1998}}</ref>

These results have been called into question due to failure of multiple researchers to replicate.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Abbott|first=A.|title=Disputed results a fresh blow for social psychology: Failure to replicate intelligence-priming effects ignites row in research community|journal=Nature|year=2013|volume=497|issue=7447|page=16|doi=10.1038/497016a |pmid=23636371|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last=Bartlett|first=T.|title=Power of suggestion The amazing influence of unconscious cues is among the most fascinating discoveries of our time – that is, if it's true.|date=30 January 2013 |url=http://chronicle.com/article/Power-of-Suggestion/136907|publisher=The Chronicle of Higher Education}}</ref>

===Implicit self-esteem (2000–present)===
{{Main|implicit self-esteem}}
Much work has been done on measuring [[self-esteem]]. More recently, there has been a focus on implicit self-esteem, and how to measure it. According to Bosson, Swann, and [[James W. Pennebaker|Pennebaker]] there is little to no [[Correlation and dependence|correlation]] between several previously used implicit self-esteem measures. The same results were found when comparing implicit and explicit measures.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Bosson|first=Jennifer K.|author2=Swann, William B. |author3=Pennebaker, James W. |title=Stalking the perfect measure of implicit self-esteem: The blind men and the elephant revisited?|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=2000|volume=79|issue=4|pages=631–643|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.79.4.631|citeseerx=10.1.1.371.9919|pmid=11045743}}</ref> Dijksterhuis acknowledges this limitation and tries to work around it by manipulating implicit self-esteem then measuring it with the [[Name–letter effect|name-letter effect]] or [[implicit-association test#Self-esteem IAT|self-esteem IAT]].  In the experiments, he uses evaluative [[Classical conditioning|conditioning]] to enhance implicit self-esteem subconsciously. The word ‘I’ was subliminally presented (15 milliseconds) followed by either positive traits (e.g. smart) or neutral words (e.g. chair), depending on the condition. Evaluative conditioning is most often used to change [[Attitude (psychology)|attitude]] towards a neutral stimuli, but he proposed it would work for implicit self-esteem.  The results support his hypothesis; participants in the experimental condition showed higher levels of implicit self-esteem on both measures compared to controls.<ref name=like>{{cite journal|last=Dijksterhuis|first=Ap|title=I Like Myself but I Don't Know Why: Enhancing Implicit Self-Esteem by Subliminal Evaluative Conditioning.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=2004|volume=86|issue=2|pages=345–355|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.86.2.345|pmid=14769089}}</ref>

[[Anthony Greenwald|Greenwald]] and Banaji’s definition of implicit self-esteem is: “implicit self-esteem is the introspectively unidentified (or inaccurately identified) effect of the self-attitude on evaluation of self-associated and self-dissociated objects”.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Greenwald|first=Anthony G.|author2=Banaji, Mahzarin R. |title=Implicit social cognition: Attitudes, self-esteem, and stereotypes.|journal=Psychological Review|year=1995|volume=102|issue=1|pages=4–27|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.102.1.4|pmid=7878162|citeseerx=10.1.1.411.2919}}</ref> Dijksterhuis proposes an alternate definition that self-esteem is the attitude and that the evaluation of objects is a consequence of this attitude. He also proposes that the different measures have not correlated with each other because they are either measuring the attitude towards self or the consequence of this attitude.<ref name="like" />

===Unconscious Thought Theory (UTT) (2002–present)===
{{Main|Unconscious thought theory}}
[[Unconscious thought theory]] (UTT) was introduced by Dijksterhuis in 2004. He presented 5 experiments showing that people make better decisions when they thought about it unconsciously rather than [[Consciousness|consciously]]. The argument is that conscious thought is not capable of handling all the complex information that we need to process in order to make good decisions. In experiment 2 participants in the conscious thought condition reported only using a subset of the information provided to make their decision.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Dijksterhuis|first=Ap|title=Think Different: The Merits of Unconscious Thought in Preference Development and Decision Making.|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=2004|volume=87|issue=5|pages=586–598|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.87.5.586|pmid=15535773|citeseerx=10.1.1.175.6744}}</ref>

In a 2006 paper, Dijksterhuis and colleagues did multiple studies looking at making the best choices. In the first study, participants were shown 4 (simple) or 12 (complex) attributes, randomized one at a time, about four fake cars. The attributes were either positive or negative. Two of the cars had equal numbers of positive and negative attributes, one was presented with 75% positive attributes, and the other 25%. After the attributes were shown participants were told they would have to pick the best car. Half the participants were allowed to think about the cars for 4 minutes (conscious thought condition) the other half were distracted for those 4 minutes solving anagrams (unconscious thought condition). After the 4 minutes everyone chose the best car and participants in the simple condition made better decisions in the conscious thought condition, but unconscious thought led to better decisions in the complex condition. Other studies are presented in the article applying this same design to the real world decisions (e.g. department store vs a furniture store).<ref>{{cite journal|last=Dijksterhuis|first=A.|title=On Making the Right Choice: The Deliberation-Without-Attention Effect|journal=Science|date=17 February 2006|volume=311|issue=5763|pages=1005–1007|doi=10.1126/science.1121629|pmid=16484496|bibcode=2006Sci...311.1005D |citeseerx=10.1.1.137.3422|s2cid=44856325 }}</ref>

Dijksterhuis wanted to get away from the artificial information that people are provided in a laboratory setting and see what happens when they have to rely on their own knowledge about a subject to make decisions. Dijksterhuis and colleagues designed an experiment where subjects had to rate their level of expertise on soccer, then pick win, lose, or draw for four upcoming soccer matches in the Dutch league. Participants were placed in one of the following conditions: choose within 20 seconds of seeing the matches, given two minutes to deliberate (conscious), or distracted for two minutes (unconscious), then asked to choose. Results show that nonexperts do about as well in all conditions but experts perform better in the unconscious thought condition. They replicated the study using World Cup matches since there is a more objective way to measure expertise with national team world ranking rather than asking participants to self-report expertise. The pattern of results was similar to the first study.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Dijksterhuis|first=Ap |author2=Bos, Maarten W. |author3=van der Leij, Andries |author4=van Baaren, Rick B.|title=Predicting Soccer Matches After Unconscious and Conscious Thought as a Function of Expertise|journal=Psychological Science|year=2009|volume=20|issue=11|pages=1381–1387|doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.2009.02451.x |pmid=19818044|s2cid=2938496 |hdl=2066/76800|hdl-access=free}}</ref>
